claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Firstly, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he is in approved education or training.

Could you tell me, is Alex currently in full-time education or training (such as A-levels, NVQs, or similar qualifications)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school but hasn't gone on to do anything else.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know.

Since Alex is 17 and has left education or training, there are still a couple of ways you might be eligible for Child Benefit. I have a few more questions:

1. Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service (such as the National Careers Service in England) or with the armed services since leaving education?

If he has done either of these, you may be able to continue receiving Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces. He's just been at home since leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

I have a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Does Alex work 24 hours or more per week in paid employment?
2. Does Alex receive any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all - maybe just the odd bit of pocket money here and there, but nothing regular. And no, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment:

1. Do you live in the UK?
2. Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information.

Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, I have to share some disappointing news.

For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit eligibility normally continues only if the child:
- Stays in **approved education or training**, OR
- Registers with a **government-sponsored careers service** or the **armed services** within a certain timeframe after leaving education (which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks)

Since Alex is 17, has left education, hasn't registered with a careers service or the armed forces, and isn't in any approved training, you would not currently qualify for Child Benefit for him.

**However**, if Alex were to:
- Start approved education or training (such as A-levels, NVQs, or a course that meets the criteria), OR
- Register with a government-sponsored careers service

...you may then become eligible to claim or re-apply for Child Benefit.

Let me now provide my formal eligibility assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment:

## Eligibility Outcome for Child Benefit

**Child: Alex (age 17)**
- **Eligible:** No

### Reasoning for Alex:
Since Alex is over 16, Child Benefit can only continue if he remains in approved education or training, or if he registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces after leaving education. As Alex has left school and is not pursuing any of these options, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for him.

### What could change this:
If Alex decides to:
- Enrol in approved education or training (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, or certain other courses), or
- Register with a government-sponsored careers service (which could allow Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks)

...you could then make a claim for Child Benefit.

I hope this information is helpful. If Alex's circumstances change, please do consider applying at that time. Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means Child Benefit eligibility depends on specific conditions being met. For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit normally continues only if the child stays in approved education or training, or if a 16 or 17 year old registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services after leaving education (allowing Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks). Alex has left school and is not in any approved education or training. He has not registered with a careers service or the armed forces. He does not work 24+ hours per week and does not receive benefits in his own right (which would have stopped eligibility anyway). Since none of the conditions for continuing Child Benefit for a child over 16 are met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives with them, meeting the basic residency requirements. However, since Alex is 17 and no longer in approved education or training, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces, the conditions required to continue Child Benefit for a child over 16 are not met. Therefore, the claimant is not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Therefore, the claimant is not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
